section.title: The Sin of Favoritism
section.sectionRationale: James takes the pure religion just defined and tests it at the church door. Faith in the glorious Lord Jesus cannot coexist with fawning over the rich and shaming the poor. He exposes favoritism as an evil-hearted judgment, shows it contradicts God's own choice of the poor, convicts it by the royal law of love, and calls his readers to speak and act under the law of liberty, where mercy triumphs.
section.thesis: To honor the rich and shame the poor is incompatible with faith in the glorious Christ; favoritism breaks the royal law of love, makes us lawbreakers, and invites judgment without mercy on the unmerciful, for mercy triumphs over judgment.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the scene of favoritism exposed; God's choice of the poor and the folly of honoring their oppressors; and the royal law that condemns partiality and calls for mercy.

verses.text: My brothers, as you hold out your faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ, do not show {{prosopolempsia|favoritism}}.
Suppose a man comes into your {{synagoge|meeting}} wearing a gold ring and fine clothes, and a poor man in shabby clothes also comes in.
If you lavish attention on the man in fine clothes and say, “Here is a seat of honor,” but say to the poor man, “You must stand” or “Sit at my feet,”
have you not discriminated among yourselves and become judges with evil thoughts?

units.label: God's choice of the poor
units.note: you honor those who oppress you

verses.text: Listen, my beloved brothers: Has not God chosen the poor of this world to be rich in faith and to inherit the kingdom He promised those who love Him?
But you have dishonored the poor. Is it not the rich who oppress you and drag you into court?
Are they not the ones who blaspheme the noble name by which you have been called?
If you really fulfill the {{nomosbasilikos|royal law}} stated in Scripture, “Love your neighbor as yourself,” you are doing well.
But if you show favoritism, you sin and are convicted by the law as transgressors.
Whoever keeps the whole law but stumbles at just one point is guilty of breaking all of it.
For He who said, “Do not commit adultery,” also said, “Do not murder.” If you do not commit adultery, but do commit murder, you have become a lawbreaker.
Speak and act as those who are going to be judged by the law that gives freedom.
For judgment without mercy will be shown to anyone who has not been merciful. {{eleos|Mercy}} triumphs over judgment.

prosopolempsia.gloss: Partiality or favoritism, literally receiving the face, judging by outward appearance. It is the sin of showing deference to some and contempt for others based on wealth, status, or looks. James insists it cannot live alongside faith in the Lord of glory, who shows no such partiality.

synagoge.gloss: A gathering or assembly, the word behind synagogue. James uses it for the Christian meeting, reflecting the Jewish background of his readers. The scene of the two visitors takes place as the church assembles, making favoritism a public, corporate sin.

nomosbasilikos.gloss: The royal law, the sovereign or kingly law, love your neighbor as yourself. It is royal because it comes from the King and rules over all other duties toward others. To keep it is to do well; to show favoritism is to break it and stand convicted as a transgressor.

eleos.gloss: Mercy, compassion shown to those in need or at fault. James warns that the unmerciful will face judgment without mercy, then lifts the eye to the gospel note: mercy triumphs over judgment. Those who have received God's mercy are marked by mercy toward others.

basic.context: James gives a vivid picture. Two men walk into the church meeting, one rich and finely dressed, one poor and shabby. If you fuss over the rich man and shove the poor man aside, James says, you have become judges with evil thoughts. It clashes with faith in our glorious Lord. And it is backwards: God has chosen the poor to be rich in faith, while it is often the rich who oppress you and slander Christ's name. Favoritism breaks the great command to love your neighbor. And since God's law is a whole, breaking it here makes you a lawbreaker. So live as people who will be judged by the law of freedom, showing mercy, because mercy triumphs over judgment.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prescriptive teaching aimed at a specific sin, partiality in the assembly. The illustration in verses 2 to 3 is a hypothetical case, not necessarily a single reported event, used to expose a real pattern. A key point is verses 10 to 11: James is not saying every sin is equally serious in its effects, but that the law is a unity given by one Lawgiver, so to break it at one point is to become a transgressor of it as a whole.
basic.originalLanguages: Favoritism is prosopolempsia, receiving the face, judging by appearances. The meeting is the synagoge, the assembly. Love your neighbor is the nomos basilikos, the royal or kingly law. And the passage climaxes in eleos, mercy, which triumphs over judgment for those who show it.
basic.typology: The royal law of neighbor-love gathers up the second table of the Ten Commandments and points to its fulfillment in Christ, who loved perfectly. God's choice of the poor to be rich in faith prefigures the great reversal of the kingdom that Jesus announced. Mercy triumphing over judgment anticipates the cross, where mercy and justice meet.
basic.christ: James calls Jesus our glorious Lord, the Lord of glory, and faith in Him leaves no room for judging by outward appearance, for He judged no one that way. Jesus embodied the royal law, loving neighbor and enemy alike, and He announced God's blessing on the poor. Supremely, at the cross mercy triumphed over the judgment we deserved, and that received mercy is meant to flow out to others.
basic.god: God shows no favoritism and has chosen the poor of this world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom. He is the one Lawgiver whose law is a unity. And He is merciful, promising that mercy triumphs over judgment, while warning that those who withhold mercy will be judged without it. His heart is set against the proud partiality that despises the lowly.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus blessed the poor and pronounced the merciful blessed, Luke 6:20 and Matthew 5:7. Love your neighbor as yourself is quoted from Leviticus 19:18. Deuteronomy 10:17 says God shows no partiality. And Jesus warns that with the measure we use it will be measured to us, Matthew 7:1-2.
basic.oneStory: God has always taken the side of the lowly and warned against judging by wealth and status, from His care for the poor in the law to Jesus blessing the poor in the Gospels. James applies this to the church: to fawn over the rich and shame the poor is to deny the character of the God who chose the poor and to break the royal law of love. The gospel answer is mercy, secured at the cross, which triumphs over judgment and reshapes how God's people treat one another.

context.literary: Chapter 2 opens a sustained argument that faith proves itself in deeds, and favoritism is the first test case, leading directly into the faith-and-works section that follows. The unit moves from illustration, verses 2 to 4, to God's contrasting choice, verses 5 to 7, to legal argument and the call to mercy, verses 8 to 13. The law of liberty in verse 12 echoes chapter 1 and frames the whole.
context.historical: In the Greco-Roman world, honor and patronage ran along lines of wealth and status, and seating in assemblies often reflected rank. James addresses churches that had imported this social sorting into their gatherings, flattering the wealthy patron and demeaning the poor believer, even though the wealthy were frequently the very ones oppressing them and slandering Christ.
context.canonical: The passage joins the Bible's consistent witness that God is impartial and defends the poor, from the law and prophets to the teaching of Jesus and the apostles. Its citation of Leviticus 19:18, the royal law, ties it to Jesus' summary of the law, and its theme of mercy over judgment reflects the gospel's own logic.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront and forbid favoritism in the church as incompatible with faith in the glorious Christ, to expose it as a violation of the royal law of love that makes one a lawbreaker, and to summon believers to live and judge by mercy under the law of liberty.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The heart of the passage is prescriptive: do not show favoritism, keep the royal law, show mercy. The two-visitor scene is an illustrative description used to convict, and verses 10 to 11 give a principle about the law's unity. The abiding command is impartial, merciful love toward neighbor.

debates.question: Does verse 10 mean all sins are equal?

views.label: No; it teaches the law's unity, not equal consequences
views.case: James's point is that the law comes from one Lawgiver and is a single whole, so to break it at any point is to stand as a transgressor of it. This does not deny that Scripture treats some sins as more serious in their effects or that some bring greater judgment.

views.label: It levels every person as guilty before God
views.case: Others stress the humbling effect: no one can claim innocence by keeping most of the law, since a single breach makes one a lawbreaker. The aim is to silence self-justification and drive us to mercy.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verse strips away any boast of partial obedience and shows favoritism to be a real breach of God's whole law, leaving us dependent on mercy.

debates.question: Who are the poor God has chosen in verse 5?

views.label: The materially poor, often rich in faith
views.case: James speaks of the poor of this world, those low in status and possessions, whom God has frequently chosen to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom. It underlines the great reversal of the gospel and the folly of despising them.

views.label: The poor as representative of the humble who trust God
views.case: Others note that throughout Scripture the poor often stand for the lowly and dependent who look to God rather than to riches. God's choice falls on such, exposing the pride of those who honor wealth.

debates.bearing: Either way the point holds: God does not sort people by wealth, and the church that fawns over the rich and shames the poor contradicts God's own gracious choice.

study.originalLanguages: The sin is named prosopolempsia, receiving the face, judging people by their outward appearance, a thing unthinkable for those who hold the faith of the Lord of glory. The setting is the synagoge, the assembly, where seats of honor and places at the feet make the partiality visible and public. Against it James sets the nomos basilikos, the royal law of the King, love your neighbor as yourself, which favoritism plainly breaks. The argument lands on eleos, mercy: the unmerciful face judgment without mercy, but mercy triumphs over judgment for those who, having received it, extend it.

typology.type: The royal law, love your neighbor
typology.fulfillment: The second table of the law summed up and fulfilled in Christ, who loved perfectly and taught this as the great command, Matthew 22:39.

typology.type: God's choice of the poor to inherit the kingdom
typology.fulfillment: The kingdom reversal announced by Jesus, blessed are the poor, Luke 6:20, and secured in Him.
The cross, where God's mercy and justice meet, so that mercy prevails for those in Christ.

study.christ: James begins with the faith of our glorious Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, and the mere mention of Him exposes favoritism as absurd, for the exalted Lord received no one by outward face and Himself came poor. Jesus lived the royal law, loving neighbor, outcast, and enemy, and He proclaimed blessing on the poor and the merciful. At the cross the deepest truth of this passage is displayed: mercy triumphed over the judgment we deserved, and all who have received that mercy are now called to show it.
study.god: God shows no partiality; the same God who forbids judging by the face has chosen the poor of this world to be rich in faith and heirs of His kingdom. He is the one Lawgiver whose commands form a single whole, so that to break one is to become a transgressor of the law. Above all He is merciful, and He binds mercy and judgment together: He warns that the unmerciful will be judged without mercy, yet proclaims that mercy triumphs over judgment for those who reflect His own compassion.

crossReferences.note: Love your neighbor as yourself, the royal law James quotes in verse 8.
God shows no partiality and takes no bribe, the divine character favoritism contradicts.
Jesus pronounces blessing on the poor, matching God's choice of the poor in verse 5.
Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy, behind mercy triumphs over judgment.
With the measure you use it will be measured to you, echoing judgment without mercy for the unmerciful.

study.oneStory: The God of Scripture has always been impartial and has always defended the poor and lowly, from the justice codes of the law to the prophets' rebukes to Jesus blessing the poor and the merciful. James sets the church's petty snobbery against this backdrop: to honor the rich and shame the poor denies the very character of God and breaks the royal law of love. The remedy is the gospel itself, for at the cross mercy triumphed over judgment, and a people who have drunk that mercy are now to pour it out, treating every neighbor as themselves.

quiz.q: Why is showing favoritism incompatible with the Christian faith?

options.text: Because it clashes with faith in the glorious Lord Jesus and makes us judges with evil thoughts.
options.note: Verses 1 and 4: partiality contradicts faith in the Lord of glory.

options.text: Because it breaks the royal law to love your neighbor as yourself.
options.note: True from verses 8 to 9, another angle on the same sin.

options.text: Because rich people should never be welcomed at all.
options.note: James forbids partiality, not the welcome of any person, rich or poor.

options.text: Because the church should simply ignore social differences.
options.note: James does not say ignore; he says do not judge people by wealth or status.

quiz.q: What has God done regarding the poor, according to verse 5?

options.text: Chosen the poor of this world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom.
options.note: Verse 5: God's gracious choice exposes the folly of despising them.

options.text: Blessed the lowly who trust Him rather than riches.
options.note: True to the sense, capturing the great reversal of the kingdom.

options.text: Promised to make every poor believer materially wealthy.
options.note: The riches in view are faith and the kingdom, not guaranteed money.

options.text: Rejected the poor in favor of the influential.
options.note: The opposite of what verse 5 says.

quiz.q: What does James mean by stumbling at one point of the law?

options.text: The law is a unity from one Lawgiver, so breaking it at one point makes you a transgressor of it.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11: the law's oneness, not equal consequences for every sin.

options.text: No one can claim innocence by keeping most of the commands.
options.note: True to the humbling effect, silencing partial-obedience boasting.

options.text: Every sin does exactly the same amount of damage.
options.note: James teaches the law's unity, not that all sins are identical in effect.

options.text: Small sins do not really count before God.
options.note: The point is the opposite: even one breach makes one a lawbreaker.

quiz.q: What does James say about mercy in verse 13?

options.text: Mercy triumphs over judgment, but the unmerciful face judgment without mercy.
options.note: Verse 13 holds both together: warning and gospel hope.

options.text: Those who show mercy will themselves receive mercy.
options.note: True to the thrust, echoing Jesus in Matthew 5:7.

options.text: Mercy means no one will ever face any judgment.
options.note: Judgment is real; mercy triumphs for the merciful, not by canceling judgment altogether.

options.text: God's judgment always overrides His mercy.
options.note: James says mercy triumphs over judgment, not the reverse.

quiz.q: What is the royal law James cites in verse 8?

options.text: Love your neighbor as yourself.
options.note: Verse 8 quotes Leviticus 19:18, the kingly law over our dealings with others.

options.text: The sovereign command from the King that governs how we treat others.
options.note: True to why it is called royal, though it names the principle rather than the words.

options.text: A rule that only applies to how we treat the wealthy.
options.note: It applies to every neighbor, which is exactly why favoritism breaks it.

options.text: The command to honor those of higher social rank.
options.note: That is favoritism, the very thing the royal law condemns.

questions: James pictures two visitors and how differently they are treated. Are there people you unconsciously honor or overlook because of appearance, wealth, or status?
God has chosen the poor to be rich in faith. How does that reshape the way you view people the world considers unimportant?
The royal law is to love your neighbor as yourself. Who is a neighbor near you that this command is calling you to love more concretely?
James says we will be judged by the law of liberty, so speak and act as merciful people. Where do you need to extend mercy rather than judgment right now?
Mercy triumphs over judgment. How does remembering the mercy you have received in Christ move you to show mercy to someone who has wronged you?

translationNotes.note: Meeting renders synagoge, literally your synagogue; a footnote notes the Greek, reflecting the Jewish setting of James's readers and their assembly.
The noble name by which you have been called can also be rendered the noble name invoked upon you, or the name of Him to whom you belong, that is, the name of Christ.
Royal law renders nomos basilikos, kingly or sovereign law; the quotation is from Leviticus 19:18, love your neighbor as yourself.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for James 2:1-13. No em or en dashes occur in this movement.
